addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai bioRxiv
An MCP server for accessing bioRxiv and medRxiv preprint servers.

This server provides:
- Tools: Search preprints, get preprint details, list subject categories
- Prompts: Analyze preprint methodology and findings

bioRxiv is a preprint server for biological sciences operated by Cold Spring
Harbor Laboratory. It hosts research manuscripts that have NOT undergone peer
review. This server wraps the official bioRxiv API to provide structured access
to preprint metadata, abstracts, and full-text PDFs.

TOOL SELECTION GUIDE:
• search_preprints: Find preprints by date range and/or subject category (no keyword, author, or full-text search) - primary discovery tool
• get_preprint: Get full details for a specific preprint by DOI
• get_categories: List available subject categories for filtering
• search_published_preprints: Find preprints that have been formally published in journals
• funder_search: Find preprints by funding source (NIH, etc.)
• get_statistics: Usage and submission statistics

MULTI-TOOL WORKFLOWS:
For comprehensive literature analysis:
1. Use search_preprints to find relevant preprints by date range and category
2. Use get_preprint for detailed information on promising results
3. Use search_published_preprints to check if preprints have been peer-reviewed and published
4. Use funder_search to identify research funding patterns in a field

For tracking research trends:
1. Use get_categories to identify relevant subject areas
2. Use search_preprints with date filters for recent activity
3. Use get_statistics to understand submission volumes

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## claude.ai Outline
Document markdown content must not begin with a top-level heading (H1) — the title is stored as a separate field, so set it via the title parameter and start the content with body text or a lower-level heading instead.

Document and collection markdown support @mentions using the syntax: @[Display Name](mention://user/userId). For example: @[John Doe](mention://user/c9a1b2e3-...). Use the "list_users" tool to find user IDs.

Read images and attachments with the "fetch" tool by setting resource to "attachment" and passing either the attachment ID or an /api/attachments.redirect?id=... URL; the tool will return a signed URL for download.

When asked to create a document that follows a template, use the "list_templates" tool to find a matching template; each result already includes the template body as markdown. To use it unchanged, pass its ID as templateId to "create_document" and the new document is pre-filled from it. To adapt it first, modify the returned body and pass the result as the text parameter to "create_document". Either way no separate fetch is needed.

attachment.content:
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I've already excluded `genomes.tar.gz` in `.gitignore`. Run these from `/work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR`:

```bash
# 1. unlock your key for this shell session
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github   # will prompt for the passphrase

# 2. stage everything (genomes/ and genomes.tar.gz are gitignored, so git skips them)
git add -A

# 3. commit
git commit -m "Add design notes, report, PDF, and BV-BRC dedup/manifest data"

# 4. push and set upstream
git push -u origin zero-shot
```

Note: `git add -A` will also pick up the modified `.gitignore` itself. If you want to double check nothing large sneaks in before pushing, run `git status` after step 2.

input.command: cd /work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR && echo "=== design notes ===" && cat "# design notes.md" && echo && echo "=== report ===" && cat "# report.md"

content.content:
=== design notes ===
# design notes
Model output: 
three classifications: 
for Antibiotic X
- likely to fail
- likely to work
- no-call (too uncertaim)
=== report ===
# report
 All 33 headers say [Staphylococcus aureus strain USFL336 | 1280.4647] — same strain, same genome_id, every single one. What looks like "different genomes" is:

One bacterial genome, split into 33 fragments (contigs).

Why a genome file has multiple > entries: sequencing doesn't read a bacterial chromosome start-to-end in one pass. It reads millions of short overlapping fragments, then software (the assembler) stitches them back together. Repetitive regions, low-coverage spots, and other gaps mean the assembler usually can't reconstruct one single continuous sequence — it reconstructs the genome in pieces instead. Each > entry here is one such piece: scaffold ERS093100SCcontig000001, ...000002, etc. — literally labeled "contig" in the header. Add them up: 33 contigs → 2,918,311 bp total, which is exactly the expected size of one S. aureus genome (~2.8–2.9 Mbp). The biggest contig is 663 kb, the rest taper down to smaller fragments — classic draft-assembly shape.

This is completely normal for public genome databases — a "complete, closed" genome (one circular chromosome, zero gaps) is actually the exception; most deposited assemblies are drafts like this one. It'd only be a real problem if headers named different strains/organisms in the same file, which isn't happening here.

Why a genome file has multiple > entries: sequencing doesn't read a bacterial chromosome start-to-end in one pass. It reads millions of short overlapping fragments, then software (the assembler) stitches them back together. Repetitive regions, low-coverage spots, and other gaps mean the assembler usually can't reconstruct one single continuous sequence — it reconstructs the genome in pieces instead. Each > entry here is one such piece: scaffold ERS093100SCcontig000001, ...000002, etc. — literally labeled "contig" in the header. Add them up: 33 contigs → 2,918,311 bp total, which is exactly the expected size of one S. aureus genome (~2.8–2.9 Mbp). The biggest contig is 663 kb, the rest taper down to smaller fragments — classic draft-assembly shape.

This is completely normal for public genome databases — a "complete, closed" genome (one circular chromosome, zero gaps) is actually the exception; most deposited assemblies are drafts like this one. It'd only be a real problem if headers named different strains/organisms in the same file, which isn't happening here.

content.text:
None of the current files alone has all three (species + dedup + antibiotic susceptibility) — they're split across three files that would need a join:

| File | Has |
|---|---|
| [staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv](bvbrc_data/staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv) | `Antibiotic` + `Resistant Phenotype` (susceptible/resistant labels) + `Genome Name` (species is in the name string), but genus-wide (all *Staphylococcus*, not filtered to aureus) and **not deduplicated** (51,209 rows) |
| [aureus_dedup_representatives.csv](bvbrc_data/aureus_dedup_representatives.csv) | The deduplicated list of *S. aureus* genome_ids (one representative per cluster), no AMR labels |
| [aureus_genome_metadata.csv](bvbrc_data/aureus_genome_metadata.csv) | `species` column + genome quality stats, no AMR labels |

To get what you're describing you'd join `staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv` (filtered to `Genome Name` containing "Staphylococcus aureus") against `aureus_dedup_representatives.csv` on `genome_id`, keeping only rows whose genome_id is in the dedup set.

Want me to build that merged CSV?

content.text:
Yes — the `Genome Name` field in `staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv` embeds the species as its first two words (e.g. `"Staphylococcus aureus 1111100370"`, `"Staphylococcus epidermidis ..."`), and I confirmed the genus TSV actually contains 10 different *Staphylococcus* species, not just aureus. So per `genome_id` you can parse species straight out of that column — you don't strictly need `aureus_genome_metadata.csv` for species.

What you still need `aureus_dedup_representatives.csv` for is the **dedup filter**: it's the only file listing which `genome_id`s survived clustering (one representative per cluster). Without it you'd keep AMR rows for redundant/near-identical genomes.

So the minimal join is just two files:
1. Take `genome_id`s from `aureus_dedup_representatives.csv`
2. Filter `staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv` to rows whose `Genome ID` is in that set (and drop rows where `Resistant Phenotype` is blank, since many are unlabeled)

Want me to write that merge script?

content.content:
# Core engine (annotate/predict/report/CLI) has NO third-party dependencies —
# it runs on pure Python 3.9+ stdlib. The packages below are only for the demo UI.
streamlit>=1.30
pandas>=1.5
---
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=61"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "genome-firewall"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Zero-shot, rule-based AMR decision-support prototype (Genome Firewall v0)."
readme = "README.md"
requires-python = ">=3.9"
license = { text = "MIT" }
authors = [{ name = "Genome Firewall team" }]
keywords = ["antimicrobial-resistance", "AMR", "biosecurity", "AMRFinderPlus", "cAMRah"]

# Core engine is dependency-free. The demo UI needs streamlit + pandas.
dependencies = []

[project.optional-dependencies]
app = ["streamlit>=1.30", "pandas>=1.5"]
dev = ["pytest>=7"]

[project.scripts]
genome-firewall = "genome_firewall.cli:main"

[tool.setuptools]
packages = ["genome_firewall"]

[tool.setuptools.package-data]
genome_firewall = ["data/*.json", "examples/*.tsv"]

content.text: pandas is available. I'll write a small script to filter to *S. aureus* rows and keep every AMR label row.

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("bvbrc_data/staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv", sep="\t", dtype=str)
species = df["Genome Name"].str.split().str[:2].str.join(" ")
aureus = df[species == "Staphylococcus aureus"].copy()
aureus = aureus.dropna(subset=["Resistant Phenotype"])
aureus = aureus[aureus["Resistant Phenotype"].str.strip() != ""]

aureus.to_csv("bvbrc_data/aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv", sep="\t", index=False)

print("rows written:", len(aureus))
print(aureus["Resistant Phenotype"].value_counts())
print("unique genomes:", aureus["Genome ID"].nunique())
EOF

content.text:
Created [bvbrc_data/aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv](bvbrc_data/aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv): 42,698 labeled rows, filtered to *S. aureus* only, keeping every row that has a `Resistant Phenotype` label (blank-label rows dropped).

- Susceptible: 30,216
- Resistant: 12,006
- Intermediate: 458
- Nonsusceptible: 18
- Covers 5,171 unique genome IDs (not deduplicated — this is all labeled rows, so genomes with multiple antibiotics tested appear multiple times)

Note this is *not* filtered against `aureus_dedup_representatives.csv`, so some near-duplicate genomes are still included. Want me to also produce a deduplicated version joined against that representative list?
<ide_opened_file>The user opened the file /work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/bvbrc_data/aureus_dedup_representatives.csv in the IDE. This may or may not be related to the current task.</ide_opened_file>
why did you create a new file?

Because that's what you asked for — "filter after aureus and **create a new tsv**" — and separately, I try not to overwrite `staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv` in place since it's the raw full-genus source data; other steps (or a re-run with different filter criteria) might still need the unfiltered original. Keeping the filtered result as a derived file (`aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv`) preserves that source.

If you'd rather I overwrite the original instead of keeping both, let me know and I'll do that going forward.

content.text:
I did delete the blank `Resistant Phenotype` rows — confirmed 0 remain in `aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv` (dropped 3,945 out of 46,643 aureus rows). If you're still seeing blank cells, that's in *other* columns, not the label column.

**What the blank cells you're seeing mean:** most of the remaining columns are optional experiment metadata that labs don't always report — e.g. `Measurement`/`Measurement Value` (the actual MIC number), `Testing Standard` (CLSI/EUCAST version used), `Vendor`, `PubMed` citation. A lab reports "Resistant" as the categorical call but often skips logging the raw MIC or which standard they used. That's normal reporting sparsity, not missing labels — the label itself (`Resistant Phenotype`) is always populated in this file now.

**On wet-lab validation** — good instinct to check. The `Evidence` column is `"Laboratory Method"` for all 42,698 rows, 100%. So yes: every row in this file is an actual in-vitro susceptibility test result (disk diffusion, broth dilution, MIC, agar dilution — see `Laboratory Typing Method`), not a computational/predicted call. BV-BRC does also store computationally-predicted AMR phenotypes elsewhere (`Evidence = "Computational Method"`), but none of those leaked into this file — the ones with blank `Resistant Phenotype` that got dropped were incomplete records, not computational predictions.

import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("bvbrc_data/staphylococcus_all_amr_lab_method.tsv", sep="\t", dtype=str)
species = df["Genome Name"].str.split().str[:2].str.join(" ")
aureus = df[species == "Staphylococcus aureus"].copy()

aureus.to_csv("bvbrc_data/aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv", sep="\t", index=False)

print("rows written:", len(aureus))
print(aureus["Resistant Phenotype"].value_counts(dropna=False))
print("unique genomes:", aureus["Genome ID"].nunique())
EOF

content.text:
Regenerated [bvbrc_data/aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv](bvbrc_data/aureus_all_amr_labels.tsv) with the full unfiltered set — all 46,643 *S. aureus* rows, species-filtered only, no rows dropped:

- Susceptible: 30,216
- Resistant: 12,006
- Intermediate: 458
- Nonsusceptible: 18
- Blank (no phenotype recorded): 3,945
- Covers 5,491 unique genome IDs

The 3,945 blank rows are still `Evidence = "Laboratory Method"` (real lab tests were run), they just have no `Resistant Phenotype` recorded — likely an antibiotic that was tested but the categorical call wasn't captured in this export, only raw measurement data or nothing at all. Worth checking their `Measurement`/`Measurement Value` columns if you want to recover a label from raw MIC values later.

prompt.text: or give me commands to do it

content.text:
Heads up before the commands: `genomes.tar.gz` is now **3.7GB**. That's over both GitHub's hard 100MB limit for regular git, *and* GitHub.com's per-file cap for Git LFS (2GB). It also blows past the free LFS storage quota (1GB free, then billed per GB). A plain LFS push of this single file will get rejected too — it needs to be split into <2GB chunks first.

```bash
# 1. un-ignore it
sed -i '/^genomes\.tar\.gz$/d' .gitignore

# 2. set up Git LFS for this repo
git lfs install
mkdir -p /work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR/.lfs-split && cd /work3/janlud/biosecurity-AMR

# 3. split into <2GB chunks (git/LFS-safe size)
split -b 1900M genomes.tar.gz genomes.tar.gz.part-

# 4. track the chunks with LFS instead of the whole file
git lfs track "genomes.tar.gz.part-*"

# 5. unlock the key (skip if already added this session)
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"
ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519_github

# 6. stage, commit, push
git add .gitattributes .gitignore genomes.tar.gz.part-*
git commit -m "Add split genomes tarball via Git LFS"
git push -u origin zero-shot

# to reassemble later after cloning:
# cat genomes.tar.gz.part-* > genomes.tar.gz
```

Note: this will still consume ~3.7GB of your LFS storage quota — check your GitHub plan's LFS data pack before pushing, or it'll fail partway with a quota error. If you'd rather not deal with LFS billing at all, hosting the tarball on something like Zenodo/S3 and committing just a download link is usually the saner path for a dataset this size — say the word and I'll set that up instead.
